narration.default: A variable is like a labelled box. You pick a name (the label), and Python keeps a value inside. You can open the box by name whenever you want what's in it. You've already made these in Scratch — clicking "Make a Variable" and typing a name creates a new labelled box.

steps.title: Put a value in the box

narration.default:
On line 1, type:

message = "Hello Python!"

This is Scratch's orange "set my variable to Hello Python!" block, written as text. The equals sign puts the value on the right INSIDE the box labelled message. From now on, the box called message holds "Hello Python!".

Box labels can use letters, numbers, and underscores. No spaces.

steps.title: Open the box and print what's inside

narration.default:
Press Enter for line 2, then type:

print(message)

In Python, print is like Scratch's say block — but instead of showing a speech bubble on the stage, it writes the message into the terminal at the bottom. And just like dragging the orange rounded variable block into a Scratch say block, in Python you put a variable into print by typing its name. So print(message) tells Python: open the box called message, then write what's inside into the terminal.

steps.title: Why no quotes around the label?

narration.default: Notice there are no quotes around the word message in the print line. That tiny detail changes everything. Without quotes, Python sees message as the name of a box and prints what's inside, which is Hello Python. If we put quotes around it instead, Python would ignore the box completely and just print the word message itself, letter by letter. The rule: no quotes means look in the box, quotes means it's just plain text.

Click Run. The terminal shows: Hello Python!

Python ran line 1 (put "Hello Python!" in the box called message) and then line 2 (opened the box and printed what was inside).
Change the text in quotes on line 1 to anything you like — your name, a joke, your favourite food. Click Run again. The print line never changes — only what's inside the box does.

steps.title: Nice — you can use variables now

narration.default: That's your first Python variable. You picked a name, put a value into the box with the equals sign, and opened the box with print. The same idea works for your name, a score, a list of friends — anything you want Python to remember and reuse.
